name: Tangible Programming Bricks
short_description: Stackable electronic LEGO-style bricks that let people build, rather than type, computer programs by physically arranging blocks that drive real devices.
long_description: Tangible Programming Bricks, developed by Timothy McNerney at the MIT Media Lab, are electronic LEGO-compatible bricks each embedding a Cricket microprocessor and ISO 'smart card' connectors on top and bottom so a stack can sense its own order and identity. Each brick can accept a parameter card that acts as a constant, timer, sensor, or other adjustable value, and the resulting physical stack runs as a program that controls real devices such as LEGO machines, sounds, and household appliances. It matters as physical AI / interaction design because it gives code a persistent, graspable form, turning programming into hands-on construction and exemplifying the tangible user interface tradition of moving computation out of the screen and into manipulable physical objects.
ai_note: The system is an embedded-microcontroller tangible interface: bricks sense their stacking order and execute the arranged program; no machine learning or AI runs in it.

images.caption: Stack of Tangible Programming Bricks with parameter cards, from McNerney's MIT Media Lab work.
images.alt_text: A vertical stack of LEGO-like electronic bricks, each with a connector on top and a small inserted parameter card, forming a physical program.

sources.title: From turtles to Tangible Programming Bricks: explorations in physical language design
sources.publisher: Personal and Ubiquitous Computing (Springer), vol. 8(5), pp. 326-337

sources.title: Tangible programming bricks: an approach to making programming accessible to everyone
sources.publisher: MIT Media Lab (MS thesis), Timothy McNerney

sources.title: From Turtles to Tangible Programming Bricks: Explorations in physical language design

interactions.title: Snap bricks into a stack, slot in a card
interactions.description: You build a program by clutching LEGO-style bricks one on top of another and pushing a parameter card into the slot on a brick's side; the two affordances are literally 'stack a brick' and 'insert a card,' and the smart-card connectors let the stack sense its own order so the assembled tower simply runs as the program.

interactions.title: Build a bike trip computer from scratch
interactions.description: A toy bicycle gets a magnet on the wheel and a Hall-effect sensor on the frame; you wire it to a TRIGGER brick, stack a CLICK brick (beeps once per wheel pass), then COUNTS/T and DISPLAY, and the tower counts revolutions over a 10-second window and shows the speed — opening up the 'black box' of a real cycle computer.

interactions.title: Debug by ear with the CLICK brick
interactions.description: Drop a CLICK brick onto a stack to hear what is flowing through it: above MEASURE it chatters rapidly, above TRIGGER it beeps only on a rising signal, and turning a KNOB card clockwise makes it click once per step — so you find bugs by listening instead of reaching for a screen or debugger.

interactions.title: Swap a card and watch the number change
interactions.description: Because CONSTANT, KNOB, SENSOR and THUMBWHEEL cards are interchangeable, a child can pop a THUMBWHEEL into a MEASURE brick, cap it with a DISPLAY, and turn it into a game — spin the digits and watch the readout track them, learning one block's behavior before assembling anything bigger.

interactions.title: Transform a data stream as it climbs the stack
interactions.description: Each brick can rewrite the values passing up through it: put a MULTIPLY brick with a '2' card in the middle of a tower and every number arriving from below is doubled before it continues upward — a 21 leaves as a 42 — so the physical order of the bricks is the computation.

interactions.title: Program everyday machines like toy cars and a microwave
interactions.description: The earliest brick experiments were aimed at end-user programming of external devices — arranging bricks to drive toy cars and to set the steps of a kitchen microwave oven — letting someone author an appliance's behavior by hand rather than through its built-in panel.

interactions.title: Invent your own brick vocabulary
interactions.description: Using the IR/BUS card you could download fresh Logo code into a brick to give it a new behavior, so a science class could mint its own domain-specific blocks — say a 'pH' or 'tilt' brick — and hand-build a microworld tailored to whatever experiment they are running that week.

interactions.title: A tabletop greenhouse data logger
interactions.description: Wire a light or temperature SENSOR card into a MEASURE brick, stack COUNTS/T and DISPLAY, and leave the tower beside a seedling so kids can read off how conditions rise and fall through the day — grounded in the same stream-of-samples bricks the bike computer already uses.
